text: The surgeon performs a central retromuscular dissection to reduce the hernia sac, including the bladder, with careful attention to the patient's left side where the bladder is significantly involved. A three-way bladder catheter is placed, and the surgeon attempts to find an areolar plane to minimize the risk of bladder injury.

The surgeon performs lateral dissection on the patient's right side, dissecting to the iliopsoas muscle and identifying the obturator canal. The preperitoneal component of the round ligament is visible. On the left side, the surgeon carefully dissects the bladder from the anterior abdominal wall and pseudosac, encountering orthopedic hardware. The orthopedic team confirms the safety of removing the loose hardware, which is then removed.

The surgeon completes bilateral myopectineal orifice dissection, exposing the hernia and reducing the bladder. The bladder is distended to check for leaks, with none observed. The surgeon begins midline placation, noting the avulsion injury of the rectus muscle off the pubis, before proceeding to transverse placation.

The surgeon performs transverse placation, similar to techniques used for direct hernias, narrowing the space by re-approximating the rectus muscle to the pubis using multiple suture lines. Visible fascia on the pubis is utilized for securing the rectus muscle.

The surgeon places overlapping mid-weight meshes to cover the groins and central portion of the lower abdominal wall. The mesh is fixated using TIS seal and permanent sutures. The flap closure is initiated using three six-inch V-lock sutures, starting from lateral to medial on both sides, with an independent suture line for the central portion.
